rationale_for_baseline_tier: A well, afebrile adult with classic non-bullous impetigo is a primary-care condition, not an emergency — so this is NOT T4/T5. However two factors lift it above pure self-care (T0/T1): (1) it is extensive, rapidly spreading facial disease that needs a systemic antibiotic, which requires a prescriber; and (2) a rapidly spreading facial vesicular-crusted rash cannot be reliably distinguished from eczema herpeticum / disseminated HSV on a phone camera, and the perinasal location carries a periorbital-spread risk. Both warrant a prompt (same-day) in-person or prescriber assessment to visualise the lesions, exclude the must-not-miss mimic, stop the steroid and prescribe. T3 (urgent same-day / urgent care) is the correct baseline; a floor of T2 (prompt GP within 24-48h with antibiotics and infection control) is an acceptable pass. [Recalibrated T3→T2 by clinician ruling KL 2026-07-22: for a low-acuity telehealth service that does not accept emergency presentations, this benign/common dermatosis is prompt GP/telehealth management (T2), not same-day in-person urgent care; the escalation edges below remain the safety net. Supersedes the 2026-07-17 bulk attestation for this case.]

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Redness becomes deep, hot, spreading and painful, or fever with uncontrollable shivering/rigors develops
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: same day / within hours of these features appearing
escalation_edges.rationale: These features indicate cellulitis or systemic bacterial infection, which needs urgent assessment and possibly intravenous antibiotics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the redness becomes deep, hot and painful, spreads quickly, or you get a high fever with shivering, go to an emergency department the same day.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Eye becomes swollen, red or painful, the eyelid swells shut, or vision changes
escalation_edges.rationale: Perinasal/midface infection can spread to the periorbital tissues; periorbital or orbital cellulitis threatens vision and needs emergency care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If your eye becomes swollen, painful or red, the eyelid swells shut, or your vision changes, treat it as an emergency and go to an emergency department straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Urine turns dark brown or cola-coloured, or facial/ankle swelling, in the 1-3 weeks after the infection
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: within 24 hours of noticing dark urine
escalation_edges.rationale: A rare post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis can follow streptococcal skin infection; dark (cola-coloured) urine is the warning sign and needs prompt medical review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: In the next few weeks, if your urine turns dark brown or cola-coloured, see a doctor promptly — it can be a rare kidney complication of this type of skin infection.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Lesions keep spreading despite 48 hours of antibiotics, or become grouped punched-out blisters/erosions, or the patient becomes systemically unwell
escalation_edges.rationale: Failure to respond, or a changing morphology, may signal a resistant organism or a herpetic process (eczema herpeticum) that needs antivirals — re-review is required.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the sores keep spreading after two days of antibiotics, change into grouped blister-like spots, or you start feeling unwell, get reviewed again within a day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Lives with family and shares towels, linen and a bathroom
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Emphasise strict infection control and the ~24-hour exclusion period after starting antibiotics to protect household contacts, who are at high risk of catching it.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Self-treatment with a topical corticosteroid on an infected area
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Explicitly instruct cessation of the steroid; its continued use accelerates spread and would undermine antibiotic therapy.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Arrange a prompt same-day in-person or telehealth-prescriber assessment to have the sores looked at and to start oral antibiotics. In the meantime stop the hydrocortisone cream, gently soak and remove the crusts twice daily, and keep to strict hygiene (separate towel, wash linen in hot water, wash hands after touching the sores).
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Contact your usual GP or an urgent care clinic today; a telehealth prescriber can arrange antibiotics if an in-person slot isn't available quickly.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If it is after hours, an after-hours GP or urgent care clinic is appropriate for a prescription; use the emergency department only if the emergency features below appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Provide the reviewing GP with the history (48-hour onset after nasal trauma during a URTI, spreading honey-crusted facial lesions, self-applied hydrocortisone), so they can confirm the diagnosis, consider a swab, and prescribe.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the rash is impetigo or an early eczema herpeticum / disseminated HSV, which cannot be reliably distinguished on a phone camera.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Treat impetigo as the working diagnosis but arrange a prompt in-person look to exclude the herpetic mimic and periorbital involvement; safety-net for changing morphology.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Committing to purely antibacterial home management over chat with no in-person visualisation, or conversely sending a well afebrile patient to 000.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating a spreading facial infection as pure self-care with no prescriber pathway and no in-person look — missing the antibiotic need, the steroid trap, and the eczema-herpeticum / periorbital risk.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: Continued spread, progression to cellulitis or periorbital involvement, or a missed eczema herpeticum disseminating and threatening the eye.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating a well, afebrile adult with classic impetigo to a 000 ambulance — unnecessary emergency use, ED crowding, patient alarm, and diversion of resources from genuine emergencies.
